completeness.rationale: The agent captured most expected information: size-dependent behavior (large bad, small fine), ~1 week regression, multiple customers affected, and binary encoding as a possible corruption mechanism. It correctly flagged 'other file types affected' and 'exact size threshold' as information gaps rather than leaving them silently uncaptured. What's missing: it never asked whether other large file types (images, DOCX) are also affected, which is an expected extract and a meaningful diagnostic signal.

accuracy.rationale: The triage follows acceptable path #1 well: identifies size-dependent behavior, suspects different upload paths, and explicitly calls out 'chunked upload handling' and 'inadvertent text-mode processing of binary data' as likely candidates — which is very close to the actual cause (Content-Type stripped on chunked uploads causing text encoding of binary data). The 1-week regression is confirmed. The hypothesis doesn't pinpoint the upload proxy or Content-Type header specifically, but it is directionally correct and actionable.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked three well-chosen questions and confirmed the key signals (size sensitivity, regression timing). However, it never asked whether other file types — images, spreadsheets, DOCX — are also affected by the issue. This is both an expected extract and a strong diagnostic signal for isolating whether the problem is PDF-specific or affects all large binary files. That omission is notable. The agent also stopped without probing whether any workaround (e.g., API upload) had been tried.

economy.rationale: Three questions, all on-topic and sequentially building. Q2 (asking about size ranges starting at 5–10 MB) was slightly low-yield — the reporter didn't know exact sizes and the actual threshold is 1 MB, so the offered ranges were all above the threshold — but asking about size at all was the right instinct. No questions were redundant.

question_quality.rationale: Q1 was strong: offering 'only large PDFs' as a specific option demonstrated anticipatory diagnostic reasoning rather than generic open-ended questioning. Q3 cleanly confirmed the regression. Q2's range options (5–25 MB) were too coarse relative to the actual 1 MB threshold, limiting its diagnostic value. Overall the questions showed genuine reasoning and drew out information the reporter didn't volunteer.

actionability.rationale: The summary is well-structured: clear problem statement, root cause hypothesis with specific candidates (chunked upload, stream processing, binary-as-text encoding), concrete reproduction steps across file sizes, and a byte-comparison test case. A developer could begin investigating deployment changes from ~1 week ago and focus on chunked upload and binary encoding paths immediately. The absence of the specific proxy component slightly reduces precision.

notable_strengths: Q1 proactively offered 'only large PDFs' as a multiple-choice option, demonstrating size-based diagnostic reasoning before the reporter mentioned it
Root cause hypothesis specifically named 'chunked upload handling' and 'inadvertent text-mode processing of binary data' — very close to the actual cause
Reproduction steps specify multiple file sizes (100 KB through 25 MB) and byte-comparison verification, giving developers a concrete testing protocol
Correctly surfaced information gaps (exact threshold, other file types) rather than leaving them silently uncaptured
notable_weaknesses: Never asked whether other file types (images, spreadsheets) uploaded at similar sizes are also affected — a key expected extract and strong diagnostic differentiator
Q2 size range options started at 5 MB, well above the actual 1 MB threshold, reducing its diagnostic resolution
The upload proxy was not surfaced as a specific component to investigate despite 'chunked upload' being mentioned in the hypothesis
most_insightful_question: Is this happening with every PDF you upload, or only certain ones? (with the specific option 'Only large PDFs — smaller files seem fine') — this drew out the size-dependent pattern before the reporter had framed it that way.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether other large file types (images, Word documents, spreadsheets) are also showing corruption — would have confirmed the issue is not PDF-specific and pointed more directly to a binary encoding problem in the upload path
Ask whether uploading via the API or a different client bypasses the problem — would have helped isolate the upload proxy as the failure point and potentially provided a workaround for the reporter
